preFix.title: Replacing a conservatory roof: what the options cost and save
preFix.subtitle: Polycarbonate, glass, solid insulated roof, or sealing it off
preFix.excerpt: A polycarbonate conservatory roof loses heat at roughly six to ten times the rate of a well-insulated wall. In winter, the room is cold and expensive to heat. In summer, it overheats. The options run from fitting new glazing to building a fully insulated solid roof. The choice depends on how you use the room and what you want to spend.
preFix.sourceNotes: LABC guidance on conservatories and building regulations (2024). Planning Portal permitted development rules for conservatories (England). BFRC window energy ratings guidance. Which? conservatory roof guide (2024). Energy Saving Trust data on conservatory heat loss.

content.text: The roof of a typical UK conservatory accounts for 70 to 80 percent of the room's total heat loss. A standard 25 mm polycarbonate panel has a
of around 2.0 to 2.8 W/m²K. Compare that to a modern cavity wall at 0.3 and a well-insulated roof at 0.18. Improving the roof is the single highest-impact upgrade available for a cold conservatory.

New 35 mm multi-wall polycarbonate reduces the U-value to around 1.3 to 1.5. High-performance conservatory glass can reach 1.0. These are improvements but the roof is still three to five times worse than a solid insulated wall. Cost: 1,500 to 3,000 pounds for a standard 3 m x 4 m conservatory. This option is most useful where planning or listed building rules prevent a solid roof.

The existing polycarbonate or glass panels are removed and replaced with a tiled or flat-roof system with 100 to 150 mm of rigid insulation. U-values reach 0.15 to 0.18. The room temperature in winter rises much. Overheating in summer is also reduced. Cost: 5,000 to 15,000 pounds depending on roof area and finish.

A conservatory is exempt from building regulations if it is separated from the house by a door and the roof is more than 75 percent glazed. Replacing the roof with a solid. Insulated roof may make the room a habitable extension under building regulations, requiring thermal compliance, fire escape. And ventilation. Check with your local building control officer before work starts.

Fit a well-insulated door or bi-fold partition between the house and the conservatory. Treat the conservatory as an unheated buffer space. This is the cheapest option if the conservatory is rarely used. The conservatory wall between house and conservatory should also be checked for insulation.
If you use the conservatory year-round and want it heated: solid insulated roof is the only option that gives a real return.
If the conservatory is used mainly in summer: upgraded glazing stops the worst overheating at lower cost.
If the conservatory is rarely used: seal it off from the house and accept it as a cold lean-to.
Listed buildings and some conservation areas: check with the local planning authority before any roof change. Solid roofs may not be permitted.

A 12 m² polycarbonate conservatory with a 10°C temperature difference loses about 240 W through the roof. Over 2,500 heating hours per year, that is 600 kWh. At 27p/kWh electricity. The annual saving from a solid roof is around 540 kWh. Worth 30 pounds at gas rates or 146 pounds at electric rates. Payback at gas rates is 40+ years; at electric rates it falls below 12 years. The numbers improve if the room is larger or the heating season longer.

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.2 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
